elements.title: Approaches to Learning
elements.source_text: Approaches to Learning

elements.title: Motivation to Learn
elements.source_text: Strand: 1.0 — Motivation to Learn

elements.title: Curiosity and Interest
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Curiosity and Interest

elements.title: Curiosity and Interest
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Express interest in some familiar and new objects, people, and activities in their immediate environment. Seek information by exploring with their senses, describing their observations, and asking simple questions.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.1 Curiosity and Interest Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Initiative
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Initiative

elements.title: Initiative
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate initiative by starting activities (such as simple play scenarios), initiating social interactions (such as helping others), and seeking solutions to problems.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.2 Initiative Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Engagement
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Engagement

elements.title: Engagement
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Actively engage by focusing and concentrating on activities for brief periods of time with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.3 Engagement Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Perseverance
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Perseverance

elements.title: Persisting Despite Difficulties
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate persistence, with adult support, when engaging in an activity despite encountering setbacks or boredom. Make an effort, with adult support, to cope with emotions that arise (for instance, frustration, sadness, anger, excitement), although may shift to another activity after a short while.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.4 Persisting Despite Difficulties Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Executive Functioning
elements.source_text: Strand: 2.0 — Executive Functioning

elements.title: Working Memory
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Working Memory

elements.title: Working Memory
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Hold approximately one to two pieces of information in their mind for a short time and use the knowledge to guide behavior when performing tasks and engaging in play, with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Working Memory Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Inhibitory Control
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Inhibitory Control

elements.title: Managing Impulsive Behaviors
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate emerging ability to manage habitual reactions (habits that are not useful in a particular context), impulsive behaviors (acting on a whim), and delaying gratification (resisting temptation for an instant reward) with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Managing Impulsive Behaviors Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Managing Attention and Distractions
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate an emerging ability to ignore distractions and interruptions during independent or group activities; however, often need adult support to manage attention when distracted or interrupted.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Managing Attention and Distractions Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Flexibility
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Flexibility

elements.title: Flexibility
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate emerging ability to shift behavior and think about things in a new way, with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.4 Flexibility Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Goal-Directed Learning
elements.source_text: Strand: 3.0 — Goal-Directed Learning

elements.title: Problem-Solving
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Problem-Solving

elements.title: Planning
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate emerging ability to set and carry out simple goals (for example, one- to two-step plans and goals), with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.1 Planning Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Reflecting and Analyzing
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Make attempts to adjust a problem-solving approach or strategy by reflecting on and analyzing their current approach, with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.2 Reflecting and Analyzing Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Collaborative Effort
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Collaborative Effort

elements.title: Problem-Solving Together
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Collaborate with peers in problem-solving during play and learning activities, sometimes for a short period of time and sometimes longer.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.3 Problem-Solving Together Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Understanding Others
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Notice, with adult support, that others have different approaches and preferences, although often have difficulty accepting others' approaches and preferences.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.4 Understanding Others Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Social and Emotional Development
elements.source_text: Social and Emotional Development

elements.title: Self
elements.source_text: Strand: 1.0 — Self

elements.title: Self-Awareness
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Self-Awareness

elements.title: Self-Identity
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.1 Self-Identity

elements.title: Approaches to Learning
elements.source_text: Approaches to Learning

elements.title: Problem-Solving
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Problem-Solving

elements.title: Planning
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Demonstrate emerging ability to set and carry out simple goals (for example, one- to two-step plans and goals), with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.1 Planning ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Demonstrate emerging ability to set and carry out simple goals

elements.title: Planning
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Demonstrate ability to set and carry out more complex plans (for example, two- to three-step plans and goals), with less adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.1 Planning ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Demonstrate ability to set and carry out more complex plans

elements.title: Reflecting and Analyzing
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Make attempts to adjust a problem-solving approach or strategy by reflecting on and analyzing their current approach, with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.2 Reflecting and Analyzing ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Reflecting and Analyzing
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Make attempts to adjust a problem-solving approach or strategy by reflecting on and analyzing their current approach, with less adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.2 Reflecting and Analyzing ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Collaborative Effort
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Collaborative Effort

elements.title: Problem-Solving Together
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Collaborate with peers in problem-solving during play and learning activities, sometimes for a short period of time and sometimes longer.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.3 Problem-Solving Together ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Problem-Solving Together
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Engage in extended collaborative problem-solving during play and learning activities. Communicate with peers about how to solve a problem and help peers when needed.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.3 Problem-Solving Together ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Understanding Others
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Notice, with adult support, that others have different approaches and preferences, although often have difficulty accepting others' approaches or preferences.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.4 Understanding Others ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Understanding Others
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Demonstrate understanding and explain that others can have different approaches and preferences and more consistently accept others' approaches and preferences, with less adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.4 Understanding Others ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Self-Awareness
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Self-Awareness

elements.title: Self-Identity
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Notice and communicate the characteristics of their physical appearance related to specific identities (for example, gender, race, ethnicity). Demonstrate emerging preferences for specific activities (for example, what to play, how to dress).
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.1 Self-Identity ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Self-Identity
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Express their personal identity (for example, gender, race, or ethnicity), including a sense of pride in their identity, and communicate preferences of their appearance or activities they enjoy (for example, sharing their family's practices or their own preferences).
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.1 Self-Identity ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Confidence in Abilities
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Describe their abilities positively and enjoy demonstrating them. Communicate the desire to be viewed positively by familiar adults, including teachers.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.2 Confidence in Abilities ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Confidence in Abilities
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Express confidence in their abilities and describe their strengths, including reference to past abilities. Continue to be sensitive to how they are viewed by peers and familiar adults, including teachers.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.2 Confidence in Abilities ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Emotional Knowledge
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Emotional Knowledge

elements.title: Understanding Emotions in Self and Others
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Identify basic emotions (for example, happy, sad, surprised) and recognize emotional expressions in self and others.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.3 Understanding Emotions in Self and Others ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Understanding Emotions in Self and Others
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Identify basic and complex emotions (for example, embarrassment, prideful) and recognize emotional expressions in self and others. Demonstrate increasing understanding of different ways of expressing emotions and related behaviors for themselves and others.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.3 Understanding Emotions in Self and Others ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Regulating Emotions and Behaviors
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Regulating Emotions and Behaviors

elements.title: Regulating Emotions, Behaviors, and Stress
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Make efforts to regulate their emotions and behaviors and cope with stress with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.4 Regulating Emotions, Behaviors, and Stress ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Regulating Emotions, Behaviors, and Stress
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Regulate emotions and behaviors and demonstrate strategies to cope with emotions and behaviors caused by stress with some adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.4 Regulating Emotions, Behaviors, and Stress ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Managing Routines and Transitions
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Manage routines and transitions between settings (for example, from home to child care) or activities (for example, from playtime to story time) with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.5 Managing Routines and Transitions ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Managing Routines and Transitions
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Anticipate routines and manage transitions between settings (for example, from home to school) or activities (for example, from snack time to small-group activities) with some adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.5 Managing Routines and Transitions ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Social Awareness
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Social Awareness

elements.title: Awareness of Similarities and Differences Across People
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate awareness and interest in how people are similar and different.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Awareness of Similarities and Differences Across People ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Awareness of Similarities and Differences Across People
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Make comparisons between themselves and others and express beliefs about themselves and others based on perceived similarities and differences.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Awareness of Similarities and Differences Across People ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Understanding Other People's Thoughts, Behaviors, and Experiences
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Develop understanding that others have unique thoughts, behaviors, and experiences.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.7 Understanding Other People's Thoughts, Behaviors, and Experiences ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Understanding Other People's Thoughts, Behaviors, and Experiences
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Demonstrate emerging understanding of the mental and psychological reasons people act as they do and how these reasons contribute to differences in how people act or behave.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.7 Understanding Other People's Thoughts, Behaviors, and Experiences ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Empathy and Caring
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Respond to others' distress and needs with sympathetic caring and assistance by comforting and helping others, although occasionally require support from an adult to assist.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.8 Empathy and Caring ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Empathy and Caring
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Demonstrate empathy by sharing the emotional experiences of others and showing concern for the needs of others in distress.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.8 Empathy and Caring ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Interactions and Relationships with Adults
elements.source_text: Strand: 2.0 — Interactions and Relationships with Adults

elements.title: Interactions with Adults
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Interactions with Adults

elements.title: Reciprocal Interactions with Adults
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Engage in positive interactions with familiar adults, especially in familiar settings. Demonstrate an emerging ability to initiate social interaction with familiar adults.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Reciprocal Interactions with Adults ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Reciprocal Interactions with Adults
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Take greater initiative and participate in more reciprocal interactions with familiar adults (for example, initiate a conversation, suggest a shared activity, or ask for assistance).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Reciprocal Interactions with Adults ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Attachment
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Attachment

elements.title: Seeking Security and Support
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years)
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Seeking Security and Support

elements.title: Social Awareness
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Social Awareness

elements.title: Awareness of Similarities and Differences Across People
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate awareness and interest in how people are similar and different. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Make comparisons between themselves and others and express beliefs about themselves and others based on perceived similarities and differences.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Awareness of Similarities and Differences Across People

elements.title: Understanding Other People's Thoughts, Behaviors, and Experiences
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate emerging understanding of the unique thoughts, behaviors, and experiences. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Develop understanding that others have mental and psychological reasons people act as they do and how these reasons contribute to differences in how people act or behave.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.7 Understanding Other People's Thoughts, Behaviors, and Experiences

elements.title: Empathy and Caring
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Respond to others' distress and needs with sympathetic caring and assistance by comforting and helping others, although occasionally require support from an adult to assist. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Demonstrate empathy by sharing the emotional experiences of others and showing concern for the needs of others in distress.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.8 Empathy and Caring

elements.title: Interactions with Adults
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Interactions with Adults

elements.title: Reciprocal Interactions with Adults
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Engage in positive interactions with familiar adults, especially in familiar settings. Demonstrate an emerging ability to initiate social interaction with familiar adults. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Take greater initiative and participate in more reciprocal interactions with familiar adults (for example, initiate a conversation, suggest a shared activity, or ask for assistance).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Reciprocal Interactions with Adults

elements.title: Attachment
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Attachment

elements.title: Coping with Departures
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Cope with departures and separations from attachment figures (for example, drop-off at preschool or family child care) but occasionally require additional assistance throughout the day to manage distress while being apart from attachment figures. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Cope with departures and separations from attachment figures and manage distress while being apart from attachment figures with minimal or no assistance.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Coping with Departures

elements.title: Relationships with Adults
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Relationships with Adults

elements.title: Relationships with Adults
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Contribute to maintaining positive relationships with attachment figures (adults in children's lives who are caregivers, which may include teachers) and familiar adults. Show emerging awareness of the adults' feelings, preferences, and well-being. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Contribute to positive mutual relationships and cooperation with attachment figures (adults in children's lives who are caregivers, which may include teachers) and familiar adults. Show interest in the adults' feelings, preferences, and well-being.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.4 Relationships with Adults

elements.title: Interactions and Relationships with Peers
elements.source_text: Strand: 3.0 — Interactions and Relationships with Peers

elements.title: Interactions with Peers
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Interactions with Peers

elements.title: Interacting and Cooperating with Peers
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Interact with peers in shared activities and occasionally participate in cooperative efforts with peers, with adult support. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): More actively and intentionally interact and cooperate with peers in daily learning and play activities.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.1 Interacting and Cooperating with Peers

elements.title: Conflict Resolution with Peers
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Seek adult assistance to resolve peer conflicts or disagreements. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Negotiate with peers and more often communicate to respond to conflict. Seek adult assistance to understand their peers' needs or to resolve a conflict.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.2 Conflict Resolution with Peers

elements.title: Equitable Social Interactions
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Equitable Social Interactions

elements.title: Fairness and Respect
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate understanding of sharing and treating those who may be similar or different from them with fairness, although require some adult prompting to share. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): More consistently share with others and treat others with fairness and respect with less adult prompting, including calling out unfairness in play and daily activities.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.3 Fairness and Respect

elements.title: Relationships with Peers
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Relationships with Peers

elements.title: Developing Friendships
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Choose to play with one or two special peers they identify as friends. Share more complex play with friends than with other children. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Develop friendships that are more reciprocal, exclusive, and enduring. Engage in enhanced cooperation and problem-solving efforts.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.4 Developing Friendships

elements.title: Foundational Language Development
elements.description: This is one out of two sub-domains in the Language and Literacy Development domain.
elements.source_text: Foundational Language Development

elements.title: Listening and Speaking
elements.source_text: Strand: 1.0 — Listening and Speaking

elements.title: Vocabulary
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Vocabulary

elements.title: Understanding and Using Vocabulary
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Understand and use words for objects, actions, and attributes frequently experienced in everyday life, such as through play, conversations, or stories. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Understand and use an increasing variety of words for objects, actions, and attributes experienced in everyday life, such as through play, conversations, or stories.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.1 Understanding and Using Vocabulary

elements.title: Understanding and Using Words for Categories
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Understand and use increasingly specific
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.2 Understanding and Using Words for Categories

elements.title: Social and Emotional Development
elements.source_text: Social and Emotional Development

elements.title: Understanding and Using Size and Location Words
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Understand and use words to describe the size and location of objects (such as "tiny" and "on"), including simple comparisons (such as "bigger"). Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Understand and use increasingly specific words to describe and compare the size and location of objects (such as "longer" and "between").
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.3 Understanding and Using Size and Location Words

elements.title: Grammar
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Grammar

elements.title: Using Grammatical Features and Sentence Structure
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Use common word forms and sentence forms to express thoughts and ideas. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Use both common and less common word forms and sentence forms to express complex thoughts and ideas. Refer to the full draft of the PTKLF for additional information on how this foundation develops depending on the languages children are learning.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.4 Using Grammatical Features and Sentence Structure*

elements.title: Asking Questions
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Use questions to seek information and to clarify and confirm understanding. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Use questions and follow-up questions to seek information and to clarify and confirm understanding.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.5 Asking Questions

elements.title: Constructing Narratives
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Use language to construct real or fictional short narratives. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Use language to construct real or fictional extended narratives that have several details or a plotline.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Constructing Narratives

elements.title: Sharing Explanations and Opinions
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Share descriptions, opinions, and explanations. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Share detailed descriptions, opinions, and explanations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.7 Sharing Explanations and Opinions

elements.title: Participating in Conversations
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Participate in back-and-forth conversations with adults and peers. Respond on topic for at least one turn in a conversation. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Participate in increasingly long and complex back-and-forth conversations with adults and peers. Respond on topic across several turns in the conversation.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.8 Participating in Conversations

elements.title: Foundational Literacy Skills
elements.source_text: Strand: 2.0 — Foundational Literacy Skills

elements.title: Phonological Awareness
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Phonological Awareness

elements.title: Isolating Initial Sounds
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Match words that have the same first sound in speech, with adult support or the support of pictures or objects. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Isolate and pronounce the first sound of a word, with adult support or the support of pictures or objects.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Isolating Initial Sounds

elements.title: Recognizing and Blending Sounds
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): When presented with two single-syllable words (such as "sand" and "box"), blend them into a compound word in speech with adult support or the support of pictures or objects. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): When presented with syllables and individual sounds, blend them into words in speech with adult support or the support of pictures or objects. Refer to the full draft of the PTKLF for additional information on how this foundation develops depending on the languages children are learning.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Recognizing and Blending Sounds*

elements.title: Participating in Rhyming and Wordplay
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Recognize or participate in familiar rhymes or songs. Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Produce rhyming sounds or words. Rhymes may be imperfect and can be real or nonsense words.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Participating in Rhyming and Wordplay

elements.title: Alphabetics and Print
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Alphabetics and Print

elements.title: Identifying Letters
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Match some letter names to their printed form. These will commonly be letters in the child's first name. If learning the alphabet in English, Spanish, or other languages using a similar alphabet, such as Tagalog, match some (about three to eight) uppercase letter names and approximately half (about 12 to 16) of the lowercase letter... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Match many letter names to their printed form. If learning the alphabet in English, Spanish, or other languages using a similar alphabet, such as Tagalog, match most (about 15 to 20) uppercase letter names and approximately half (about 12 to 16) of the lowercase letter...
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.4 Identifying Letters*

elements.title: Isolating Initial Sounds
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Match words that have the same first sound in speech, with adult support or the support of pictures or objects.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Isolating Initial Sounds ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Match words that have the same first sound in speech, with adult support or the support of pictures or objects.

elements.title: Isolating Initial Sounds
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Isolate and pronounce the first sound of a word, with adult support or the support of pictures or objects.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Isolating Initial Sounds ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Isolate and pronounce the first sound of a word, with adult support or the support of pictures or objects.

elements.title: Recognizing and Blending Sounds
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): When presented with two single-syllable words (such as "sand" and "box"), blend them into a compound word in speech with adult support or the support of pictures or objects.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Recognizing and Blending Sounds*

elements.title: Recognizing and Blending Sounds
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): When presented with syllables and individual sounds, blend them into words in speech with adult support or the support of pictures or objects.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Recognizing and Blending Sounds*

elements.title: Participating in Rhyming and Wordplay
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Recognize or participate in familiar rhymes or songs.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Participating in Rhyming and Wordplay

elements.title: Participating in Rhyming and Wordplay
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Produce rhyming sounds or words. Rhymes may be imperfect and can be real or nonsense words.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Participating in Rhyming and Wordplay

elements.title: Identifying Letters
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Match some letter names to their printed form. These will commonly be letters in the child's first name. If learning the alphabet in English, Spanish, or other languages using a similar alphabet, such as Tagalog, match some (about three to eight) uppercase letter names to their printed form.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.4 Identifying Letters*

elements.title: Identifying Letters
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Match many letter names to their printed form. If learning the alphabet in English, Spanish, or other languages using a similar alphabet, such as Tagalog, match most (about 15 to 20) uppercase letter names and approximately half (about 12 to 16) of the lowercase letter names to their printed form.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.4 Identifying Letters*

elements.title: Learning Letter-Sound Correspondence
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Recognize that letters or characters have sounds.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.5 Learning Letter-Sound Correspondence

elements.title: Learning Letter-Sound Correspondence
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Accurately identify or produce sounds associated with several letters or common characters with adult support. If learning the alphabet in English, Spanish, or other languages using a similar alphabet, such as Tagalog, accurately identify or produce sounds associated with about half of the letters.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.5 Learning Letter-Sound Correspondence

elements.title: Concepts About Print
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Concepts About Print

elements.title: Understanding the Concept of Print
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Identify the meaning of a few instances of familiar print in the environment.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.6 Understanding the Concept of Print

elements.title: Understanding the Concept of Print
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Recognize print as something that can be read and has meaning.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.6 Understanding the Concept of Print

elements.title: Understanding Print Conventions
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Display basic book-handling behaviors and knowledge of basic print conventions, such as turning pages in a single direction and recognizing the cover and title of a book.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.7 Understanding Print Conventions

elements.title: Understanding Print Conventions
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Display increasingly sophisticated book-handling behaviors and knowledge of print conventions, such as turning pages one at a time and understanding the direction and orientation of print.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.7 Understanding Print Conventions

elements.title: Reading
elements.source_text: Strand: 3.0 — Reading

elements.title: Literacy Interest and Response
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Literacy Interest and Response

elements.title: Demonstrating Interest in Literacy Activities
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate interest in and engagement with literacy and literacy-related activities.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.1 Demonstrating Interest in Literacy Activities

elements.title: Demonstrating Interest in Literacy Activities
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Demonstrate interest in and engagement with literacy and literacy-related activities for progressively extended periods of time and with increasing independence.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.1 Demonstrating Interest in Literacy Activities

elements.title: Comprehension and Analysis of Age-Appropriate Text
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Comprehension and Analysis of Age-Appropriate Text*

elements.title: Understanding Stories
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Demonstrate basic understanding of main characters or events in a story after the child has experienced the story a few times.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.2 Understanding Stories

elements.title: Understanding Stories

elements.title: Language and Literacy Development
elements.source_text: This is one out of two sub-domains in the Language and Literacy Development domain.

elements.title: Understanding Words
elements.description: Vocabulary. Discovering: Pay attention to English oral language and understand a few common English words, while relying mainly on intonation, facial expressions, and gestures of the speaker in interactions with adults and peers. Developing: Demonstrate understanding of words in English for objects and actions as well as phrases encountered frequently in interactions with adults and peers. Broadening: Demonstrate understanding of a larger set of words in English (for example, objects and actions, personal pronouns, possessives, and descriptive terms) in interactions with adults and peers.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.1 Understanding Words

elements.title: Using Words
elements.description: Vocabulary. Discovering: Use English words, mainly consisting of concrete nouns. Developing: Use varied English words, including an increasing number of concrete nouns and some verbs and pronouns. Broadening: Use a wide variety of English words to share knowledge of concepts, including words across all parts of speech, with some inaccuracies.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.2 Using Words

elements.title: Using Grammatical Features
elements.description: Grammar. Discovering: Use one or two familiar English verbs as all-purpose verbs, with some inaccuracies. Developing: Use some grammatical rules in English, such as using -s or -es for plural nouns and -ing for verbs, sometimes with inaccuracies. Broadening: Broaden the use of English grammatical rules, such as irregular plurals or simple past tense verbs, sometimes with inaccuracies.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.3 Using Grammatical Features

elements.title: Using Complex Sentence Structures
elements.description: Grammar. Discovering: Use words or phrases repeatedly experienced in English to communicate. Developing: Use a few formulaic English sentence structures to communicate on a range of topics by switching out key words in a phrase. Broadening: Use a variety of English grammatical forms, with some inaccuracies, to produce many different types of sentence structures. Grammatical forms can include adding appropriate possessive pronouns (for example, your, my); conjunctions (for example, and, or); and other elements (for example, adjectives, adverbs).
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.4 Using Complex Sentence Structures

elements.title: Communicating Needs
elements.description: Language Use. Discovering: Show increasing reliance on nonverbal communication with English speakers, such as gestures or behaviors, to seek attention, make a request, or initiate a response. Developing: Combine nonverbal communication and some English phrases to be understood by English speakers. Broadening: Use single English words and verbal communication in English to be understood by English speakers.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.5 Communicating Needs

elements.title: Understanding Requests and Directions
elements.description: Language Use. Discovering: Follow simple directions in English when given additional contextual clues. Developing: Follow directions that involve repeatedly experienced routines or contextual clues. Broadening: Follow directions in English that involve more than one step, relying less on contextual clues.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Understanding Requests and Directions

elements.title: Asking Questions
elements.description: Discovering: Use a frequently experienced question phrase (such as "What's that?") or use one or two English words with a rising pitch to ask questions. Developing: Use a few question structures as a formula, filling in different words to ask about various topics. Broadening: Use "who," "what," "why," "how," "when," and "where" to produce questions in many forms to ask about a variety of topics in English.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.7 Asking Questions

elements.title: Constructing Narratives
elements.description: Discovering: Identify parts of real or fictional narratives using a few English vocabulary words. Developing: Construct real or fictional short narratives using English vocabulary in a few simple English sentence structures. Broadening: Construct real or fictional narratives by stringing together sentences with varied structures in English.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.8 Constructing Narratives

elements.title: Sharing Explanations and Opinions
elements.description: Discovering: Use English vocabulary and gestures to share descriptions, opinions, and explanations. Developing: Use a few simple sentence structures to share descriptions, opinions, and explanations. Broadening: Use varied sentence structures to share descriptions, opinions, and explanations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.9 Sharing Explanations and Opinions

elements.title: Participating in Conversations
elements.description: Discovering: Engage in conversation with English speakers, by mostly listening and responding with a few English words, gestures, or other nonverbal responses. Developing: Converse with others using frequently heard vocabulary in English, often using short, commonly used sentences and phrases and a few repeated grammatical structures, sometimes with inaccuracies. Broadening: Engage in conversations in English using increasingly complex vocabulary and varied grammatical structures, sometimes with inaccuracies.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.10 Participating in Conversations

elements.title: Foundational Literacy Skills
elements.source_text: Strand: 2.0 — Foundational Literacy Skills

elements.title: Recognizing and Segmenting Sounds
elements.description: Phonological Awareness. Discovering: Recognize and produce sounds of spoken English. Developing: Isolate and pronounce the first sound of a word in English, with adult support or the support of pictures or objects. Broadening: Match English words that have the same first sound in speech, with adult support or the support of pictures or objects.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Recognizing and Segmenting Sounds

elements.title: Recognizing and Blending Sounds
elements.description: Phonological Awareness. Discovering: Recognize and produce sounds of spoken English. Developing: When presented with two syllables and individual sounds, blend them into English words in speech, with adult support or the support of pictures or objects. Broadening: When presented with single-syllable English words (such as "sand" and "box"), blend them into a compound word in speech, with adult support or the support of pictures or objects.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Recognizing and Blending Sounds

elements.title: Participating in Rhyming and Wordplay
elements.description: Phonological Awareness. Discovering: Repeat or recite parts of simple songs, poems, and fingerplays. Developing: Attend to and participate in frequently experienced songs, poems, and fingerplays. Broadening: Repeat, recite, produce, or initiate simple songs, poems, and fingerplays.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Participating in Rhyming and Wordplay
