elements.title: Mathematics
elements.source_text: Mathematics

elements.title: Counting and Cardinality
elements.source_text: Strand: 1.0 — Counting and Cardinality

elements.title: Counting Principles
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Counting Principles

elements.title: Reciting Numbers
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Recite numbers in order from one to ten or higher with no more than a few errors.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.1 Reciting Numbers — Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Reciting Numbers
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Recite numbers in order from one to thirty with no more than a few errors. Count forward from a number other than one.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.1 Reciting Numbers — Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: One-to-One Correspondence
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Count five objects or more using one-to-one correspondence (one object for each number word).
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.2 One-to-One Correspondence — Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: One-to-One Correspondence
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Count ten objects or more using one-to-one correspondence (one object for each number word).
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.2 One-to-One Correspondence — Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Cardinality
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Answer the question "How many?" by counting. May repeat the last number word in the number list after counting but is still developing an understanding that the number name of the last object counted represents the total number of objects in the group.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.3 Cardinality — Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Cardinality
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Consistently demonstrate understanding when counting that the number name of the last object counted represents the total number of objects in the group.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.3 Cardinality — Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Recognizing Quantities
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Recognizing Quantities

elements.title: Subitize
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Identify without counting the number of objects in a small collection (for example, one to four objects).
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.4 Subitize — Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Subitize
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Identify without counting the number of objects in a collection of one to five objects.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.4 Subitize — Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Numeral Recognition
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Numeral Recognition

elements.title: Numeral Recognition
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Recognize and name a few written numerals under 10.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.5 Numeral Recognition — Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Numeral Recognition
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Recognize and name all written numerals through 10.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.5 Numeral Recognition — Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Number Relationships
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Number Relationships

elements.title: Number Comparison
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Compare two groups of objects that are clearly equal or different in size and communicate, "same" or "more."
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Number Comparison — Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Number Comparison
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Compare (with or without counting) two groups of objects by counting and communicating, "more," "same," "less," or "fewer."
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Number Comparison — Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Operations and Algebraic Thinking
elements.source_text: Strand: 2.0 — Operations and Algebraic Thinking

elements.title: Number Operations
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Number Operations

elements.title: Principles of Addition and Subtraction
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate understanding that adding one or taking away one object changes the number in a small group of objects by exactly one.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Principles of Addition and Subtraction — Early (3 to 4 ½ Years)

elements.title: Principles of Addition and Subtraction
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Demonstrate understanding that adding or taking away one or more objects from a group will increase or decrease the number of objects in the group.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Principles of Addition and Subtraction — Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Number Composition and Decomposition
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate understanding that a set of objects is made up of smaller parts and that the whole set is bigger than its parts. Combine two small sets to create a larger set (for example, 3 and 2 to make a set of 5).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Number Composition and Decomposition — Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Number Composition and Decomposition
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Decompose a set of objects into two small sets in more than one way (for example, decompose 5 into sets of 3 and 2, or 1 and 4).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Number Composition and Decomposition — Later (4 to 5 ½ Years)

elements.title: Solving Addition and Subtraction Problems
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Solve addition and subtraction problems with a very small number of objects in the context
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Solving Addition and Subtraction Problems — Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Solving Addition and Subtraction Problems
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Solve addition and subtraction problems with a larger number of objects (sums up to 10) in the context
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Solving Addition and Subtraction Problems — Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years)

elements.title: Numeral Recognition
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Recognize and name a few written numerals under 10.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.5 Numeral Recognition ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Recognize and name a few written numerals ... under 10.

elements.title: Numeral Recognition
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Recognize and name all written numerals through 10.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.5 Numeral Recognition ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Recognize and name all written numerals through 10.

elements.title: Number Comparison
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Compare two groups of objects that are clearly equal or different in size and communicate, "same" or "more."
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Number Comparison ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Compare two groups of objects that are clearly equal or different in size and communicate, "same" or "more."

elements.title: Number Comparison
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Compare (with or without counting) two groups of objects by counting and communicating, "more," "same," "less," or "fewer."
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Number Comparison ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Compare (with or without counting) two groups of objects by counting and communicating, "more," "same," "less," or "fewer."

elements.title: Number Operations
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Number Operations

elements.title: Principles of Addition and Subtraction
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate understanding that adding one or taking away one object changes the number in a small group of objects by exactly one.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Principles of Addition and Subtraction ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Demonstrate understanding that adding one or taking away one object changes the number in a small group of objects by exactly one.

elements.title: Principles of Addition and Subtraction
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Demonstrate understanding that adding or taking away one or more objects from a group will increase or decrease the number of objects in the group.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Principles of Addition and Subtraction ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Demonstrate understanding that adding or taking away one or more objects from a group will increase or decrease the number of objects in the group.

elements.title: Number Composition and Decomposition
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Demonstrate understanding that a set of objects is made up of smaller parts and that the whole set is bigger than its parts.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Number Composition and Decomposition ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Demonstrate understanding that a set of objects is made up of smaller parts and that the whole set is bigger than its parts.

elements.title: Number Composition and Decomposition
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Decompose a set of objects into two small sets in more than one way (for example, decompose 5 into sets of 3 and 2, or 1 and 4). Combine two small sets to create a larger set (for example, 3 and 2 to make a set of 5).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Number Composition and Decomposition ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Decompose a set of objects into two small sets in more than one way ... Combine two small sets to create a larger set

elements.title: Solving Addition and Subtraction Problems
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Solve addition and subtraction problems with a very small number of objects in the context of everyday situations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Solving Addition and Subtraction Problems ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Solve addition and subtraction problems with a very small number of objects in the context of everyday situations.

elements.title: Solving Addition and Subtraction Problems
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Solve addition and subtraction problems with a larger number of objects (sums up to 10) in the context of everyday situations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Solving Addition and Subtraction Problems ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Solve addition and subtraction problems with a larger number of objects (sums up to 10) in the context of everyday situations.

elements.title: Sharing Objects (Division)
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Share a small number of objects (for example, four or six objects) equally between two recipients.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.4 Sharing Objects (Division) ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Share a small number of objects (for example, four or six objects) equally between two recipients.

elements.title: Sharing Objects (Division)
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Share a slightly larger number of objects equally between two or more recipients (for example, nine objects among three recipients).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.4 Sharing Objects (Division) ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Share a slightly larger number of objects equally between two or more recipients (for example, nine objects among three recipients).

elements.title: Classifying and Patterning
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Classifying and Patterning

elements.title: Sorting and Classifying
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Notice similarities and differences in the attributes of objects. Sort and classify objects by one attribute into two or more groups.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.5 Sorting and Classifying ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Notice similarities and differences in the attributes of objects. Sort and classify objects by one attribute into two or more groups.

elements.title: Sorting and Classifying
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Sort and classify objects by one or more attributes into two or more groups with accuracy and flexibility. When sorting by two attributes, a child may first sort by one attribute and then by the second attribute.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.5 Sorting and Classifying ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Sort and classify objects by one or more attributes into two or more groups with accuracy and flexibility.

elements.title: Recognizing, Duplicating, and Extending Patterns
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Notice and explore patterns in their environment and, with adult support, duplicate simple repeating patterns (for example, ABAB).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.6 Recognizing, Duplicating, and Extending Patterns ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Notice and explore patterns in their environment and, with adult support, duplicate simple repeating patterns (for example, ABAB).

elements.title: Recognizing, Duplicating, and Extending Patterns
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Explore, extend, and duplicate a variety of repeating patterns (for example, AABBAABB, ABCABC) with adult support. Describe the repeating part of a pattern (pattern unit).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.6 Recognizing, Duplicating, and Extending Patterns ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Explore, extend, and duplicate a variety of repeating patterns (for example, AABBAABB, ABCABC) with adult support. Describe the repeating part of a pattern (pattern unit).

elements.title: Creating Patterns
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Create, with adult support, a simple repeating pattern (for example, ABAB).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.7 Creating Patterns ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Create, with adult support, a simple repeating pattern (for example, ABAB).

elements.title: Creating Patterns
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Create a variety of repeating patterns (for example, AABBAABB, ABCABC) or recreate existing patterns using different objects.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.7 Creating Patterns ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Create a variety of repeating patterns (for example, AABBAABB, ABCABC) or recreate existing patterns using different objects.

elements.title: Measurement and Data
elements.source_text: Strand: 3.0 — Measurement and Data

elements.title: Comparing and Ordering Objects
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Comparing and Ordering Objects

elements.title: Comparing Measurable Attributes of Objects
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate awareness that objects can be compared by length, weight, or capacity by noticing differences in objects and communicating about their comparison.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.1 Comparing Measurable Attributes of Objects ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Demonstrate awareness that objects can be compared by length, weight, or capacity by noticing differences in objects and communicating about their comparison.

elements.title: Comparing Measurable Attributes of Objects
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Compare two objects by length, weight, or capacity (for example, putting objects side by side) and communicate about their comparison.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.1 Comparing Measurable Attributes of Objects ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Compare two objects by length, weight, or capacity (for example, putting objects side by side) and communicate about their comparison.

elements.title: Ordering Objects
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Order a few objects (for example, three) by length or other attributes (for example, height, capacity).
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.2 Ordering Objects ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Order a few objects (for example, three) by length or other attributes (for example, height, capacity).

elements.title: Ordering Objects
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Order a slightly larger number of objects (for example, four or five) by length or other attributes (for example, height, capacity).
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.2 Ordering Objects ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Order a slightly larger number of objects (for example, four or five) by length or other attributes (for example, height, capacity).

elements.title: Measuring Length
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Measure length using concrete objects laid end to end, sometimes needing adult support. Note: Children may not yet understand that units need to be of equal length. (Early: No foundation)
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.3 Measuring Length ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) (No foundation) ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) Measure length using concrete objects laid end to end, sometimes needing adult support.

elements.title: Data
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Data

elements.title: Representing Data
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Use objects, tally marks, or pictographs to represent data in two groups, with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.4 Representing Data ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Use objects, tally marks, or pictographs to represent data in two groups, with adult support.

elements.title: Representing Data
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Use objects, tally marks, or pictographs to represent data in two or more groups. Demonstrate understanding that each object, tally mark, or picture represents one data point.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.4 Representing Data ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Use objects, tally marks, or pictographs to represent data in two or more groups. Demonstrate understanding that each object, tally mark, or picture represents one data point.

elements.title: Interpreting Data
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Notice, with adult support, differences in the number of data points in two or more groups. Determine which group has more or less.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.5 Interpreting Data ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Notice, with adult support, differences in the number of data points in two or more groups. ... Determine which group has more or less.

elements.title: Interpreting Data
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Describe and compare, with adult support, the data of two groups and describe which one has more or less.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.5 Interpreting Data ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Describe and compare, with adult support, the data of two groups and describe which one has more or less.

elements.title: Geometry and Spatial Thinking
elements.source_text: Strand: 4.0 — Geometry and Spatial Thinking

elements.title: Shapes
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Shapes

elements.title: Identifying Two-Dimensional Shapes
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Identify familiar two-dimensional shapes such as circle, square, triangle, and rectangle. Note: May not identify a nontypical version of a shape (for example, a square turned so that the point is down—a diamond).
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.1 Identifying Two-Dimensional Shapes ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Identify familiar two-dimensional shapes such as circle, square, triangle, and rectangle.

elements.title: Identifying Two-Dimensional Shapes
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Identify, describe, and construct different shapes including variations of circle, square, triangle, rectangle, and other shapes. Use informal language to describe defining properties of a shape (for example, sides, corners, round).
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.1 Identifying Two-Dimensional Shapes ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Identify, describe, and construct different shapes including variations of circle, square, triangle, rectangle, and other shapes.

elements.title: Identifying Three-Dimensional Shapes
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.2 Identifying Three-Dimensional Shapes

elements.title: Ordering Objects
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Order a few objects (for example, three) by length or other attributes (for example, height, capacity).
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.2 Ordering Objects ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Order a few objects (for example, three) by length or other attributes (for example, height, capacity).

elements.title: Ordering Objects
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Order a slightly larger number of objects (for example, four or five) by length or other attributes (for example, height, capacity).
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.2 Ordering Objects ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Order a slightly larger number of objects (for example, four or five) by length or other attributes (for example, height, capacity).

elements.title: Measuring Length
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): (No foundation)
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.3 Measuring Length ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... (No foundation)

elements.title: Measuring Length
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Measure length using concrete objects laid end to end, sometimes needing adult support. Note: Children may not yet understand that units need to be of equal length.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.3 Measuring Length ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Measure length using concrete objects laid end to end, sometimes needing adult support. Note: Children may not yet understand that units need to be of equal length.

elements.title: Representing Data
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Use objects, tally marks, or pictographs to represent data in two groups, with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.4 Representing Data ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Use objects, tally marks, or pictographs to represent data in two groups, with adult support.

elements.title: Representing Data
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Use objects, tally marks, or pictographs to represent data in two or more groups. Demonstrate understanding that each object, tally mark, or picture represents one data point.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.4 Representing Data ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Use objects, tally marks, or pictographs to represent data in two or more groups. Demonstrate understanding that each object, tally mark, or picture represents one data point.

elements.title: Interpreting Data
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Notice, with adult support, differences in the number of data points in two or more groups. Determine which group has more or less.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.5 Interpreting Data ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Notice, with adult support, differences in the number of data points in two or more groups. Determine which group has more or less.

elements.title: Interpreting Data
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Describe and compare, with adult support, the data of two groups and describe which one has more or less.
elements.source_text: Foundation 3.5 Interpreting Data ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Describe and compare, with adult support, the data of two groups and describe which one has more or less.

elements.title: Shapes
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Shapes

elements.title: Identifying Two-Dimensional Shapes
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Identify familiar two-dimensional shapes such as circle, square, triangle, and rectangle. Note: May not identify a nontypical version of a shape (for example, a square turned so that the point is down-a diamond).
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.1 Identifying Two-Dimensional Shapes ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Identify familiar two-dimensional shapes such as circle, square, triangle, and rectangle.

elements.title: Identifying Two-Dimensional Shapes
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Identify, describe, and construct different shapes including variations of circle, square, triangle, rectangle, and other shapes. Use informal language to describe defining properties of a shape (for example, sides, corners, round).
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.1 Identifying Two-Dimensional Shapes ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Identify, describe, and construct different shapes...

elements.title: Identifying Three-Dimensional Shapes
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Occasionally identify a few familiar three-dimensional shapes using informal names (for example, saying "ball" when referring to a sphere).
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.2 Identifying Three-Dimensional Shapes ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Occasionally identify a few familiar three-dimensional shapes using informal names

elements.title: Identifying Three-Dimensional Shapes
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Identify a few familiar three-dimensional shapes such as sphere, cube, and cylinder. Note: Sometimes still use informal names (for example, ball, square box, tube).
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.2 Identifying Three-Dimensional Shapes ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Identify a few familiar three-dimensional shapes such as sphere, cube, and cylinder.

elements.title: Comparing Two-Dimensional Shapes
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Compare two-dimensional shapes of different sizes and orientations to determine whether they are the same shape.
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.3 Comparing Two-Dimensional Shapes ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Compare two-dimensional shapes of different sizes and orientations to determine whether they are the same shape.

elements.title: Comparing Two-Dimensional Shapes
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Compare two-dimensional shapes of different sizes and orientations to determine whether they are the same shape. Identify similarities and differences in the properties (number of sides or vertices) of two different shapes.
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.3 Comparing Two-Dimensional Shapes ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Compare two-dimensional shapes... Identify similarities and differences in the properties

elements.title: Composing Shapes
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Use two- or three-dimensional shapes to represent different elements of a picture or design (for example, adding a circle in a corner to represent the sun).
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.4 Composing Shapes ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Use two- or three-dimensional shapes to represent different elements of a picture or design

elements.title: Composing Shapes
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Combine different two- or three-dimensional shapes to create a picture or design (for example, make a house with two blocks shaped like rectangular prisms and one shaped like a triangular prism).
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.4 Composing Shapes ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Combine different two- or three-dimensional shapes to create a picture or design

elements.title: Spatial Thinking
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Spatial Thinking

elements.title: Positions and Directions in Space
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Identify some positions of objects and people in space such as in/on, under/over, up/down, and inside/outside.
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.5 Positions and Directions in Space ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Identify some positions of objects and people in space such as in/on, under/over, up/down, and inside/outside.

elements.title: Positions and Directions in Space
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Identify positions of objects and people in space including in/on, under/over, up/down, inside/outside, near/far, next to, beside/between, and in front of/behind.
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.5 Positions and Directions in Space ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Identify positions of objects and people in space including in/on, under/over, up/down, inside/outside, near/far, next to, beside/between, and in front of/behind.

elements.title: Mental Rotation
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Rely on trial and error to determine how objects move in space and fit in different locations (for example, try to fit an object into a hole by rotating, flipping, or sliding the piece in different orientations until it fits).
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.6 Mental Rotation ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Rely on trial and error to determine how objects move in space and fit in different locations

elements.title: Mental Rotation
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Rotate, flip, or slide objects to solve a problem without relying as much on physical trial and error (for example, rotate an object before fitting it into a hole).
elements.source_text: Foundation 4.6 Mental Rotation ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Rotate, flip, or slide objects to solve a problem without relying as much on physical trial and error

elements.title: Science
elements.source_text: Science

elements.title: Science and Engineering Practices
elements.source_text: Strand: 1.0 — Science and Engineering Practices

elements.title: Observation and Investigation
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Observation and Investigation

elements.title: Making Observations
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Observe and actively explore objects and events using their senses and describe their observations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.1 Making Observations ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Observe and actively explore objects and events using their senses and describe their observations.

elements.title: Making Observations
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Observe and actively explore objects and events using their senses and describe their observations in greater detail.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.1 Making Observations ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Observe and actively explore objects and events using their senses and describe their observations in greater detail.

elements.title: Comparing and Contrasting
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Compare and contrast objects and events and describe similarities and differences based on observable properties.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.2 Comparing and Contrasting ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Compare and contrast objects and events and describe similarities and differences based on observable properties.

elements.title: Comparing and Contrasting
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Compare and contrast objects and events based on physical properties and functions and describe similarities and differences in greater detail.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.2 Comparing and Contrasting ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Compare and contrast objects and events based on physical properties and functions and describe similarities and differences in greater detail.

elements.title: Asking Questions
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Demonstrate curiosity and raise simple questions about objects and events in their environment.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.3 Asking Questions ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Demonstrate curiosity and raise simple questions about objects and events in their environment.

elements.title: Asking Questions
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Demonstrate curiosity and an increased ability to formulate specific and detailed questions about objects and events in their environment.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.3 Asking Questions ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Demonstrate curiosity and an increased ability to formulate specific and detailed questions about objects and events in their environment.

elements.title: Defining Problems
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Identify problems during play and everyday interactions and try simple solutions on their own or in collaboration with peers and adults.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.4 Defining Problems ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Identify problems during play and everyday interactions and try simple solutions on their own or in collaboration with peers and adults.

elements.title: Defining Problems
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Identify problems during play and everyday interactions and try multistep solutions on their own or in collaboration with peers and adults.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.4 Defining Problems ... Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years) ... Identify problems during play and everyday interactions and try multistep solutions on their own or in collaboration with peers and adults.

elements.title: Making Predictions
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Make simple predictions, give simple reasons for their predictions, and, with adult support, check the predictions through concrete experiences.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.5 Making Predictions ... Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years) ... Make simple predictions, give simple reasons for their predictions, and, with adult support, check the predictions through concrete experiences.

elements.title: Making Predictions
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Make more detailed predictions drawing on prior experiences and observations, create plans with adult support to check predictions, and demonstrate an emerging ability to discuss why predictions were correct or incorrect.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.5 Making Predictions ... Later (4 to 5 ½ Years) ... Make more detailed predictions drawing on prior experiences and observations, create plans with adult support to check predictions

elements.title: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Carry out simple experiments or [text truncated]
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Planning and Carrying Out Investigations ... Early (3 to 4 ½ Years) ... Carry out simple experiments or

elements.title: Observation and Investigation
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - Observation and Investigation

elements.title: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Carry out more complex experiments or investigations, on their own or in collaboration with peers and adults, with greater persistence. Use observations and results of prior explorations to generate new questions and test their hypotheses.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.6 Planning and Carrying Out Investigations

elements.title: Using Tools
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Identify and use some observation and measurement tools, with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.7 Using Tools

elements.title: Using Tools
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Identify and more spontaneously use a greater variety of observation and measurement tools, with some adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.7 Using Tools

elements.title: Documentation, Analysis, and Communication
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Documentation, Analysis, and Communication

elements.title: Documenting Observations and Using Models
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Record observations or findings with adult support and use simple representations, including drawings, models, movement, role-play, and other methods, to convey their observations and understanding of science concepts.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.8 Documenting Observations and Using Models

elements.title: Documenting Observations and Using Models
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Record observations or findings in greater detail with some adult support and use more elaborate representations, including drawings, models, charts, diagrams, movement, role-play, and other methods, to convey their observations and understanding of science concepts.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.8 Documenting Observations and Using Models

elements.title: Mathematical Thinking and Analyzing Data
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Use mathematical thinking to analyze and quantify their observations and answer questions that arise in everyday activities, with adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.9 Mathematical Thinking and Analyzing Data

elements.title: Mathematical Thinking and Analyzing Data
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Use mathematical thinking with greater precision to analyze and quantify their observations and answer questions that arise in everyday activities, with some adult support.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.9 Mathematical Thinking and Analyzing Data

elements.title: Formulating and Communicating Explanations and Solutions
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Formulate and communicate simple explanations and solutions during play and collaborative investigations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.10 Formulating and Communicating Explanations and Solutions

elements.title: Formulating and Communicating Explanations and Solutions
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Formulate and communicate more detailed and precise explanations and solutions during play and collaborative investigations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 1.10 Formulating and Communicating Explanations and Solutions

elements.title: Physical Science
elements.source_text: Strand: 2.0 — Physical Science

elements.title: Properties and Characteristics of Nonliving Objects and Materials
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Properties and Characteristics of Nonliving Objects and Materials

elements.title: Characteristics of Objects and Materials
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 ½ Years): Investigate and describe the characteristics and physical properties of objects and solid or nonsolid materials (for example, size, weight, shape, color, texture, smell, and sound).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Characteristics of Objects and Materials

elements.title: Characteristics of Objects and Materials
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Investigate and describe in greater detail the characteristics and physical properties of objects and solid, liquid, or gas materials (for example, size, weight, shape, color, texture, smell, and sound).
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.1 Characteristics of Objects and Materials

elements.title: Light and Sound Waves
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Notice and explore sound, light, and shadows using their senses and by manipulating objects and materials during play and collaborative investigations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Light and Sound Waves

elements.title: Light and Sound Waves
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Explore and describe changes in the properties of sound, light, and shadows by manipulating different objects and materials during play and collaborative investigations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.2 Light and Sound Waves

elements.title: Changes in Nonliving Objects and Materials
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand - - Changes in Nonliving Objects and Materials

elements.title: Exploring Changes in Objects and Materials
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Explore and describe changes in objects and materials using their senses (for example, change in color, shape, texture, temperature) during play and collaborative investigations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Exploring Changes in Objects and Materials

elements.title: Exploring Changes in Objects and Materials
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 1/2 Years): Explore, describe in greater detail, and explain changes in objects and materials using their senses (for example, change in color, shape, texture, form, temperature) during play and collaborative investigations.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.3 Exploring Changes in Objects and Materials

elements.title: Force and Motion
elements.description: Early (3 to 4 1/2 Years): Observe and describe the way objects' speed and direction change and explore the effect of their own actions (for example, pushing, pulling, rolling, dropping) on making objects move.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.4 Force and Motion

elements.title: Force and Motion
elements.description: Later (4 to 5 ½ Years): Make and test predictions about how objects change direction, speed, or the distance they go and, based on their observations, explain why objects start, stop, or change direction.
elements.source_text: Foundation 2.4 Force and Motion

elements.title: Properties and Characteristics of Nonliving Objects and Materials
elements.source_text: Sub-Strand — Properties and Characteristics of Nonliving Objects and Materials
